A lab adopts a model as an explanation generator. They notice it can produce “I have it” with great conviction. Because that confidence signal tracks the story of discovery too well, weak hypotheses are repeatedly promoted: the model’s certainty makes them look better than their actual support warrants.

To compensate, the lab imposes layered filters for explanation quality. First a plausibility screen, then coherence, then fit to evidence, then elegance. This seems sensible if explanation quality decomposes cleanly into such layers. But the lab also knows an uncomfortable fact: some historically important ideas would have failed early screens when judged by the standards of their time. So the filter stack has a structural risk. It may suppress exactly those proposals that matter because they first appear confused, ugly, or wrong by prevailing criteria.

Now the lab tries to resolve a philosophical dispute. One faction says the model need not understand anything; it only has to generate candidate explanations. Another faction still wants the model to produce explanations that “reach” beyond the inputs. But if reaching beyond the inputs means more than recombining surfaces that already look plausible, then some structure is needed that is not identical to surface plausibility. So the two demands pull against each other: denying understanding while requiring explanatory reach leaves an unresolved gap. Either “reach” is being used weakly, meaning only stylistic extension, or the model must possess some nontrivial organization that functionally resembles understanding in at least this limited role.

During one evaluation session, the graduate mutt chewed the thread while muttering about Soviet penalties. Taken as true, this event is part of the same world as the methodological debate. A physical or conceptual thread was damaged during the process, and the muttering introduced irrelevant but real noise into the environment. The evaluators must therefore reason under contamination: some signal path or line of reasoning may have been interrupted while distracting language persisted alongside it.

Afterwards, trim control over the inexplicable smuggled goods returned. So there had previously been such control, it had lapsed, and then it was restored. The restoration is only trim control, not full command, and it concerns goods that remain inexplicable even after recovery of control. In this world, then, the institution does not end with clean understanding. It ends with partial governance over items whose presence or movement cannot be satisfactorily accounted for.

Putting it together, the lab’s epistemic situation is unstable. Confidence is inflated, filters may reject breakthroughs, “reach” cannot be demanded without smuggling in structure, the process is literally or figuratively disrupted by thread-chewing noise, and the final achievement is merely partial control over unexplained cargo. The world implied by these premises is one where selection mechanisms look orderly but are vulnerable at every stage: by overconfident narration, by conservative standards, by hidden requirements for structure, by interference, and by the fact that restored control does not equal understanding.
